question: Describe the role of the Program Counter (PC), Instruction Register (IR), and Memory Address Register (MAR) during the fetch phase of the instruction cycle.
expectedPoints: The Program Counter (PC) holds the memory address of the next instruction to be fetched.
This address from the PC is loaded into the Memory Address Register (MAR) to initiate a memory read operation.
The instruction word returned from memory is then stored in the Instruction Register (IR) for decoding.
Finally, the PC is incremented to point to the next sequential instruction in memory.

question: Explain the process of executing a 'Load' instruction (e.g., LD R1, [1000]) from fetch to completion. Detail the role of the data path components involved.
expectedPoints: Fetch: The address from the PC is used to fetch the LD instruction from memory into the IR. The PC is then incremented.
Decode: The control unit decodes the instruction in the IR, identifying it as a Load that requires a memory address operand (1000).
Execute: The effective address (1000) is computed (if necessary) and placed into the MAR.
Memory Access: A read signal is sent to memory. The data from address 1000 is fetched and placed into the MDR (Memory Data Register).
Writeback: The data from the MDR is written into the destination register R1 via the internal CPU bus.

question: Compare and contrast the Hardwired Control Unit and the Microprogrammed Control Unit. Discuss the key advantages and disadvantages of each approach in terms of design complexity, flexibility, and performance.
expectedPoints: Hardwired Control: Uses combinational and sequential logic circuits (decoders, counters, gates) to generate control signals. It is faster due to its direct hardware implementation, leading to higher potential performance.
Microprogrammed Control: Uses a sequencer and a control store (ROM) holding microinstructions. Each machine instruction is executed by sequencing through a set of microinstructions.

Complexity: Hardwired design is more complex and difficult to design, modify, and debug for a large instruction set. Microprogrammed design is more systematic and easier to modify by updating the microcode.

Flexibility: Microprogrammed control is highly flexible; new instructions can be added by writing new microcode without changing hardware. Hardwired control is rigid and inflexible after manufacturing.

Performance: Hardwired control has less overhead (no microinstruction fetch cycles) and is typically faster. Microprogrammed control introduces an extra layer of interpretation, which can slow down execution.
